verses.fhb_text: Therefore, having been justified by faith, we have peace with our Father through our Lord Jesus Christ,
verses.decision_notes:
Headline: Theos distinguished from 'our Lord Jesus Christ' — Father identification by the NT 'X of God' / Father-Son contrast pattern.

Renderings:
• "peace with our Father" — pros ton theon resolves to Father per FHB-1.2-1 (Father distinguished from Son in the same clause)
• "our Lord Jesus Christ" — Pauline confessional form preserved per FHB-1.2-20

Greek: pros ton theon dia tou kuriou hēmōn Iēsou Christou → "with our Father through our Lord Jesus Christ"

verses.fhb_text: through whom also we have obtained our access by faith into this grace in which we stand, and we rejoice in hope of the glory of our Father.
verses.decision_notes:
Headline: 'glory of God' resolves to 'glory of our Father' per the NT 'X of God' construction (FHB-1.2-1) — the Father is distinguished from the Son who provides access.

Renderings:
• "the glory of our Father" — tēs doxēs tou theou per NT X-of-God general clause; the glory hoped for is the Father's, accessed through the Son

verses.fhb_text: And not only that, but we also rejoice in our sufferings, knowing that suffering produces endurance,
verses.decision_notes: Straightforward Pauline argument; no FHB-distinctive Person-resolution required.

verses.fhb_text: and endurance produces character, and character produces hope;
verses.decision_notes: Continuation of v.3 chain; no FHB-distinctive choice.

verses.fhb_text: and hope does not put us to shame, because our Father's love has been poured out into our hearts through the Holy Spirit who has been given to us.
verses.decision_notes:
Headline: Trinitarian moment — the Father's love is delivered into the heart by the Holy Spirit (the Son is in view from v.6 onward).

Renderings:
• "our Father's love" — hē agapē tou theou per NT X-of-God; Father distinguished from Spirit in the same clause
• "the Holy Spirit who has been given to us" — post-Pentecost Pauline context per FHB-1.2-7(D); pneumatos hagiou tou dothentos hēmin preserved with the definite-article force
• "does not put us to shame" — kataischunei rendered with the modern 'put to shame' idiom; ESV/NIV agree

Greek: hē agapē tou theou ekkechutai en tais kardiais hēmōn dia pneumatos hagiou → "our Father's love has been poured out into our hearts through the Holy Spirit"

verses.fhb_text: For while we were still weak, at the right time Christ died for the ungodly.
verses.decision_notes: 'Christ' preserved per FHB-1.2-20 Greek convention; opens the Adam-Christ section.

verses.fhb_text: For one will scarcely die for a righteous person, though perhaps for a good person one would even dare to die.
verses.decision_notes: Headline: Paul's rhetorical aside — commas chosen over em dash per FHB-9.5-1 (the pause is gentle, not dramatic).

verses.fhb_text: But our Father shows his own love for us in this: while we were still sinners, Christ died for us.
verses.decision_notes:
Headline: The Father-heart center of Romans 5 — the Father proves his love through the Son's death.

Renderings:
• "our Father shows his own love for us" — ho theos sunistēsin tēn heautou agapēn per FHB-1.2-1 (Father distinguished from Christ in the same verse); heautou (own) preserved to retain Paul's emphasis on the love being our Father's
• "in this:" — colon rendering of the hoti clause to set up the parallel action that follows; modern English rhythm per FHB-9.13-1
• "Christ died for us" — Christos huper hēmōn apethanen preserved

Greek: sunistēsin de tēn heautou agapēn eis hēmas ho theos hoti... Christos huper hēmōn apethanen → "our Father shows his own love for us in this: ... Christ died for us"
verses.footnote: Our Father proves his love at the cross — by giving his Son for us while we were still far from him.

verses.fhb_text: Much more then, having now been justified by his blood, we will be saved through him from the wrath.
verses.decision_notes:
Headline: 'shall' modernized to 'will' per FHB-9.10-1.

Renderings:
• "by his blood" — en tō haimati autou preserved; en as instrumental per FHB-7.11-1
• "from the wrath" — apo tēs orgēs; Greek does not supply 'God's' here, so FHB does not pad. The wrath is the Father's, but Paul's word is the bare definite article

verses.fhb_text: For if while we were enemies we were reconciled to our Father through the death of his Son, much more, having been reconciled, we will be saved by his life.
verses.decision_notes:
Headline: Reconciliation as filial restoration — to the Father, by the Son.

Renderings:
• "reconciled to our Father" — katēllagēmen tō theō per FHB-1.2-1 (Father explicitly distinguished from 'his Son' in the same verse)
• "the death of his Son" — tou thanatou tou huiou autou preserved
• "by his life" — en tē zōē autou; the antecedent of autou is the Son (Christ's resurrection life)

Greek: katēllagēmen tō theō dia tou thanatou tou huiou autou → "reconciled to our Father through the death of his Son"

verses.fhb_text: And not only that, but we also rejoice in our Father through our Lord Jesus Christ, through whom we have now received the reconciliation.
verses.decision_notes:
Headline: 'rejoice in God through our Lord Jesus Christ' — Father distinguished from Christ; both named.

Renderings:
• "rejoice in our Father" — kauchōmenoi en tō theō per FHB-1.2-1
• "through our Lord Jesus Christ" — dia tou kuriou hēmōn Iēsou Christou preserved per FHB-1.2-20

verses.fhb_text: Therefore, just as sin entered the world through one man, and death through sin, and so death spread to all people, because all sinned —
verses.decision_notes:
Headline: Opening of the Adam-Christ typology; em dash preserves Paul's broken syntax (aposiopesis) — he picks up the thought in v.13 and resumes the comparison in v.18 per FHB-9.5-1 / FHB-9.8-1.

Renderings:
• "all people" — pantas anthrōpous rendered with modern collective 'people'; calque 'children of humanity' avoided per FHB-7.12-1

verses.fhb_text: for sin was in the world before the law was given, but sin is not counted where there is no law.
verses.decision_notes: Parenthetical clarifying clause within Paul's broken comparison.

verses.fhb_text: Yet death reigned from Adam to Moses, even over those whose sinning was not like the transgression of Adam, who is a type of the one to come.
verses.decision_notes: Headline: Adam as 'type of the one to come' — Christ as antitype. Preserved verbatim; no apposition needed in body since 'the one to come' is Paul's deliberate forward-pointing phrase.

verses.fhb_text: But the free gift is not like the trespass. For if many died through one man's trespass, much more have our Father's grace and the free gift in the grace of the one man Jesus Christ abounded for many.
verses.decision_notes:
Headline: Father-Son cooperation — the Father's grace given through the grace of Jesus Christ.

Renderings:
• "our Father's grace" — hē charis tou theou per FHB-1.2-1 (Father distinguished from 'the one man Jesus Christ' in the same clause)
• "the free gift in the grace of the one man Jesus Christ" — hē dōrea en chariti tē tou henos anthrōpou Iēsou Christou preserved

Greek: hē charis tou theou kai hē dōrea en chariti tē tou henos anthrōpou Iēsou Christou → "our Father's grace and the free gift in the grace of the one man Jesus Christ"

verses.fhb_text: And the free gift is not like the result of that one man's sin. For the judgment following one trespass brought condemnation, but the free gift following many trespasses brought justification.
verses.decision_notes: Headline: Krima vs charisma — judgment vs gift. Preserved Pauline contrast.

verses.fhb_text: For if, because of one man's trespass, death reigned through that one man, much more will those who receive the abundance of grace and the free gift of righteousness reign in life through the one man Jesus Christ.
verses.decision_notes: Adam's trespass → death's reign; the redeemed's life → reign in Jesus Christ. Standard rendering.

verses.fhb_text: So then, as one trespass led to condemnation for all people, so also one act of righteousness leads to justification and life for all people.
verses.decision_notes: Headline: Paul's parallel restated cleanly after the v.13-17 parenthesis. 'Therefore' opens v.12; 'So then' opens v.18 to mark the resumed argument per FHB-9.3-1.

verses.fhb_text: For just as by the one man's disobedience the many were made sinners, so by the one man's obedience the many will be made righteous.
verses.decision_notes: Headline: Adam's disobedience and Christ's obedience as the two pivot-points of human standing.

verses.fhb_text: Now the law came in to increase the trespass, but where sin increased, grace abounded all the more,
verses.decision_notes: Headline: The famous Pauline reversal — grace outruns sin. Modern phrasing per FHB-9.9-1.

verses.fhb_text: so that, as sin reigned in death, grace also might reign through righteousness leading to eternal life through Jesus Christ our Lord.
verses.decision_notes: Headline: Chapter-closing doxological cadence — 'Jesus Christ our Lord' preserved as the Pauline confessional form per FHB-1.2-20.

chapter_notes: Romans 5 is the Father-heart center of Paul's gospel: peace with our Father (v.1), the glory of our Father (v.2), our Father's love poured into us by the Holy Spirit (v.5), our Father proving his love at the cross (v.8), reconciled to our Father through the death of his Son (v.10), rejoicing in our Father through our Lord Jesus Christ (v.11), and our Father's grace abounding through Jesus Christ (v.15). Every Theos in the chapter is distinguished from the Son in the same clause and resolves to 'our Father' per FHB-1.2-1's NT 'X of God' general clause. The Adam-Christ typology (vv.12-21) is preserved without apposition — Paul names 'one man' / 'one trespass' / 'one act of righteousness' as the structural device, then names Jesus Christ explicitly at vv.15, 17, 21. One footnote at v.8 anchors the Father-heart reading of the cross.
